name: Keycloak
abstract: Open-source (Apache 2.0) IAM/CIAM by Red Hat. Zero licensing cost with unlimited users. Most feature-rich open-source option with full SPI extensibility. Requires self-hosting and DevOps expertise.

featureList.description: 12+ built-in providers plus any custom OIDC or SAML identity provider.
WebAuthn passwordless and two-factor authentication.
Email OTP for passwordless authentication.
Phone OTP via custom SPI extension or third-party provider. No built-in SMS gateway.
Magic link via custom authenticator SPI. Not available out-of-the-box. The p2-inc/keycloak-magic-link extension is the most widely used community solution.
Keycloak requires user identity for authentication. Anonymous/guest sessions are not a built-in feature.
HOTP (counter-based OTP) supported alongside TOTP.
WebAuthn security keys for U2F-style authentication.
SMS OTP via SPI extension. No built-in SMS provider.
Recovery Codes are a built-in required action since Keycloak 22+. Single-use backup codes generated at MFA enrollment.
No built-in push notification MFA. Community extensions exist (e.g. ba-itsys/keycloak-push-mfa-extension) but are not officially maintained.
No built-in adaptive/risk-based MFA. Can be implemented via custom SPI or community extensions such as mabartos/keycloak-adaptive-authn.

Cisco Duo provides official SSO integration via OIDC/SAML. For direct authenticator-level integration, the community-maintained instipod/DuoUniversalKeycloakAuthenticator extension (tested with Keycloak 26.x) is available.
WS-Federation is not natively supported. A community extension (cloudtrust/keycloak-wsfed) exists but is not officially maintained or vetted by the Keycloak team.
OAuth 2.0 client credentials grant natively supported.
Full LDAP and Active Directory federation with read/write sync.
Azure AD/Entra ID federation via OIDC or SAML identity brokering.
Import users via Admin REST API (POST /admin/realms/{realm}/users), Partial Import API, or LDAP sync.
Import password hashes via Admin API with custom hashing algorithm support.
Export users via Admin REST API or realm export.
Bulk operations via Admin REST API.
Partial Import API supports OVERWRITE, SKIP, and FAIL conflict strategies, enabling upsert behavior.

Keycloak accepts a broad Unicode range via regex but username character handling is version-dependent. Spaces, CJK characters, and special symbols have caused regressions between versions (e.g., v23→v24). Import success depends on Keycloak version and specific characters involved.
TOTP secrets can be imported via the JSON realm import format or the admin REST API using the credentials array with fields: secret (BASE32), digits, period, and algorithm.
No built-in SCIM support. Community extensions are available.
JIT user provisioning during first federation login.
Lazy migration via User Storage SPI for gradual migration from existing user stores.
Keycloak Account Console for self-service profile, MFA, and session management.
Link multiple identity providers to a single Keycloak account.
Disable user accounts to prevent authentication via the Admin Console or Admin REST API.
Custom user attributes stored as user attributes in Keycloak.
Client attributes (custom metadata per client/application) can be stored via the Admin REST API.

Attribute value limits are database-level constraints, not formally documented in official docs. Unmanaged attributes support up to ~2048 characters in recent versions (extended from the historical 255-char limit). Limits are deployment-dependent.
Realm roles and client roles with composite roles support.
Organizations feature (since v26.0) and Realms for full tenant isolation.
Username validation via user profile validators (regex, length) configurable per realm.
Progressive profiling supported since Keycloak v24.0.
Attribute-based access control via Keycloak Authorization Services.
Resource, scope, and policy-based authorization via Keycloak Authorization Services.
Keycloak is open-source software. Compliance depends on the operator's deployment.
Keycloak is open-source software. Compliance depends on the operator's deployment and infrastructure.
Keycloak itself is software. HIPAA compliance depends on how it is deployed and operated.
Keycloak is open-source software. PCI DSS compliance depends on the operator's deployment and infrastructure.
Keycloak is open-source software. CSA STAR compliance depends on the operator's deployment and infrastructure.
Keycloak is open-source software that can be deployed in FedRAMP-authorized cloud environments.
Export user data via Admin REST API.
Delete user accounts and associated data via Admin REST API (DELETE /admin/realms/{realm}/users/{id}) or Admin Console.
OAuth 2.0 consent screen and scope management built-in.
Self-hosted deployment anywhere with cross-datacenter replication support.
Self-host on AWS (EC2, EKS, ROSA), Azure (VMs, AKS), GCP, or any other cloud.
Event listener SPI supports syslog, JSON, and Gelf log handlers.
Encryption at rest via database-level Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) or filesystem/block-device encryption. Operator-dependent; Keycloak itself does not encrypt data at rest by default.
TLS/HTTPS is supported and configurable. Keycloak recommends TLS for all traffic.
Manage your own encryption keys via the deployment infrastructure.

Built-in Google reCAPTCHA support for registration flows (v2 and v3, including reCAPTCHA Enterprise). Login flow CAPTCHA requires a community extension. Third-party privacy-friendly CAPTCHA providers (Friendly Captcha, ALTCHA) are available via extensions.

Brute force detection locks accounts per user after repeated failures. Native per-IP throttling across multiple accounts is not built-in; a reverse proxy or WAF is recommended for IP-level rate limiting.

No built-in HaveIBeenPwned integration. Community extensions are available (e.g. alvinbaena/keycloak-pwned-password-policy). Keycloak does support a password blacklist feature that can use the HIBP list.

IP allow/deny lists are not built into the core Quarkus-based Keycloak distribution. This capability was removed from the legacy distribution. Per-realm IP filtering is available via deployment infrastructure (reverse proxy, WAF) or some managed Keycloak hosting providers.
Per-realm (tenant) FreeMarker theming for custom branding.
Per-realm MFA policies and authentication flows.
Direct grant API allows credential-based flows for embedded auth. Hosted redirect flow is standard.
Full FreeMarker theming enables complete brand customization.
20+ built-in language packs with custom translation support.
Account Console and configurable Login theme served by Keycloak.
Events API for login analytics. External dashboards (Grafana, ELK) recommended.
Keycloak adapters for Java (Spring Boot, Quarkus), JavaScript, Node.js, Go, Python, PHP. Keycloak SDKs via community.
No built-in IP-based rate limiting. Brute-force detection handles per-user lockout. External WAF or reverse proxy required for true rate limiting.
Full SPI extensibility: custom authenticators, identity providers, user federation, event listeners, token mappers, and more.
Keycloak SPIs are implemented in Java. No JavaScript/TypeScript runtime for extensibility.
Deploy on any custom domain via reverse proxy configuration.
kcadm.sh CLI for configuration management and CI/CD pipelines.
Community-maintained Terraform provider: mrparkers/keycloak.
User Storage SPI for connecting to any external user store.
Event listener SPI supports custom HTTP webhooks for authentication events.
Full FreeMarker templating for all login page customization.
Configure custom SMTP server in realm settings.
FreeMarker email templates for all transactional emails.
Protocol mappers and scripted mappers for custom token claims.
Authentication flows are configured via the admin console but are not a visual drag-and-drop builder.
No built-in identity proofing or document verification. Can be integrated via custom SPI or third-party identity verification services.
No built-in support for decentralized identifiers (DIDs), verifiable credentials, or self-sovereign identity.
Keycloak is an open-source IAM platform with no built-in billing or subscription management features.

Keycloak serves as an OAuth 2.1/OIDC authorization server for MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers. MCP spec 2025-03-26 is fully supported; MCP 2025-06-18 and later are partially supported (missing RFC 8707 Resource Indicators). OAuth Client ID Metadata Document support is experimental.
